title: audit-only — persistent FIFO docker-exec pattern has no single-cycle integration target
headline: 13b's 25-50× speedup is real but applies to docker-exec floor, not bridge_health.sh's host-local hexa invocations — there is no in-scope integration; daemonization is required and is out of scope for one cycle.
summary: Phase B-2 of runner-13 was conceived as integrating the persistent FIFO docker-exec pattern (proven in 13b at 25-50× speedup) into tool/bridge_health.sh's 16-bridge sweep, with target ≥10× sweep wall speedup. On audit the integration target collapses: bridge_health.sh runs `$HEXA_BIN run $SCRIPT --selftest` LOCALLY on the host (L235 of tool/bridge_health.sh), not via `docker exec`. The HEXA_REMOTE_DOCKER=1 mode at scripts/bin/hexa_remote L1228 is for a different code path (hexa_remote SSH transport with optional container isolation), and hexa_remote is invoked once per `hexa run` call — meaning persistent shell across calls would need a daemon (PID file, lifecycle, lock, recovery, auth), which is non-trivial and exceeds a single PSC cycle's scope. No nexus commit is produced this cycle.

predecessors.omega_runner_13b: proved persistent FIFO pattern reaches p50=1-3ms vs bare 49-95ms (25-50× speedup)

context.phase_b_2_brief: Integrate persistent FIFO pattern into bridge_health.sh's 16-bridge sweep; target ≥10× wall speedup.
context.audit_finding: The integration target is misidentified. The 13b speedup applies ONLY to docker-exec invocation floor — that is, when EVERY call goes through `docker exec -i $C sh -lc ...`. bridge_health.sh does not. Substituting persistent-FIFO for the bridge sweep would require either (a) routing each bridge through hexa_remote in HEXA_REMOTE_DOCKER=1 mode, which is not how the sweep is built, or (b) building a host-side hexa-interp daemon, which is a different optimization (amortize ~35ms hexa startup) with no 13b proof point.

evidence.claim: bridge_health.sh invokes hexa LOCALLY (not via docker)
evidence.implication: $HEXA_BIN is the host hexa binary ($HOME/core/hexa-lang/hexa or /usr/local/bin/hexa). No docker exec involved. Per-call floor is hexa-interp startup (~35ms host-measured), not docker-exec floor.

evidence.claim: hexa_remote's docker-exec mode fires `docker exec -i $C bash -lc ...` per invocation, but hexa_remote itself is invoked once per `hexa run X` — no cross-invocation state
evidence.implication: Within a single hexa_remote call there is exactly one docker exec — persistent mode adds zero value. Cross-call persistence requires a daemon, not a per-call coproc.

evidence.method: 3 successive `/Users/ghost/core/hexa-lang/hexa --version` runs measured via python time.time()*1000 around the call
evidence.implication: 16 bridges × 35ms = 560ms host-side hexa startup overhead in a sweep. A host-side hexa daemon could amortize this — but it's a different optimization than 13b proved (which was docker-exec floor, ~80ms).

evidence.claim: `hexa batch` subcommand is documented in --help but not implemented
evidence.help_excerpt: batch <f1> <f2> ...     Run N scripts sharing one CLI dispatch\n                              Each result delimited by ===BATCH_RESULT===
evidence.implication: If `hexa batch` were implemented, bridge_health.sh could feasibly call `hexa batch <16 selftest scripts>` and amortize startup naturally. This would be a clean integration target — but implementing `hexa batch` is itself a feature project (lives in hexa-lang, not nexus), not a runner-cycle deliverable.

option_1_audit_only_close.rationale: Brief's integration target is ill-posed for one cycle. Honest closure now is more valuable than over-stretching to fabricate an integration.

candidates_examined.name: bridge_health.sh --strict SHA256 batch verify
candidates_examined.reason_rejected: Pure local file ops (shasum). No docker exec involved. Persistent mode does not apply.

candidates_examined.name: hexa-interp persistent session (daemon-style)
candidates_examined.reason_rejected: Different optimization (amortize hexa startup ~35ms, not docker-exec floor ~80ms). Has NO proof point — 13b did not measure this. Would also require interpreter-side state isolation between bridges, which the current `hexa run` model does not provide. Greenfield design, not integration.

candidates_examined.name: hexa_remote with HEXA_REMOTE_DOCKER + cross-invocation daemon
candidates_examined.reason_rejected: Requires PID-file lifecycle, lock, restart, auth, recovery. Exceeds a single PSC cycle's scope per brief instruction `Don't over-stretch.`

option_3_proof_of_concept_daemon_tool.rationale: A new tool/runner_persistent_daemon.hexa demonstrating the daemon pattern without integration would duplicate 13b's existing FIFO POC at tool/runner_exec_bench.hexa without adding new evidence. 13b already shows the savings; the gap is integration, and integration requires lifecycle/PID/lock work that is one cycle on its own.

responsibilities: On first request: spawn persistent hexa-interp child with FIFO request/response pipes
Per request: send `run <script>` line, read until ===HEXA_DONE=== sentinel
Liveness: PID file at $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/hexa-daemon.pid, fcntl lock to prevent duplicate spawn
Recovery: if daemon dies mid-session, fallback to fresh `hexa run`; auto-respawn on next call
Stale cleanup: detect stale PID via kill(pid, 0); reap and respawn
Auth: optional shared-secret in env/config for multi-user host (default: file-permission-based on FIFO socket)

components.concern: Between two `run <script>` invocations on the same daemon, all globals/imports/env from script-1 must not leak into script-2. Current `hexa run` model assumes process-level isolation; daemonization invalidates that assumption.
options: Fork-per-request (eliminates state-leak but loses startup savings — defeats purpose)
Module-system reset hook in hexa interp (clean re-init of globals/imports each request — requires hexa-lang change)
Sandbox each request in a fresh AST/eval context within one process (most engineering work, biggest payoff)

components.delta: Replace L235 fork-per-bridge with a single `hexa-daemon dispatch <16-script-list>` call returning ===HEXA_RESULT=== delimited stdout/stderr/EC tuples; parse and dispatch to existing classify/append-history logic.

must_show: No false-positive PASS due to state leak (run --strict twice, confirm consistent SHA verifies)
No false-negative FAIL due to daemon-side error masking
Daemon survives container restart / hexa upgrade (lifecycle robustness)

what_a_real_integration_would_require.estimate: 1 dedicated cycle for hexa-daemon + isolation; 1 cycle for bridge_health.sh integration + validation. Cannot be a sub-task of runner-13c.

files_created.reason: anima closure JSONs are untracked per PSC convention

validation.step: confirm bridge_health.sh selftest path is host-local hexa
validation.result: no docker references; HEXA_BIN is host-resolved (L52-69)

validation.step: confirm hexa_remote docker mode is per-invocation only
validation.result: only HEXA_REMOTE_DOCKER (no persistent-mode hooks); per-call docker exec at L1237
34, 35, 36 ms — bounds the sweep amortization opportunity at ~16 × 35 = 560ms, distinct from docker-exec floor
error: unknown subcommand 'batch' — confirms no in-tree batching primitive exists

scope_constraints_honoured: no edit to parallel-session-locked files: cli/run.hexa, state/atlas_health_timeline.jsonl, bisociation/spectra/g_atlas_composite_v3.json, tool/omega_cycle_atlas_ingest.hexa, n6/*, tool/kick_*.hexa, design/kick/*, .own, tool/audit_shim_ssot.hexa — none touched
no commit in anima — closure JSON written as untracked
no commit in nexus — audit-only finding has no code delta to commit (option 1 chosen)
CLI-only inter-call respected — no library imports introduced
hexa-only-suffix N/A this cycle — no new tools created
no `git add -A` / `git add .` — no nexus staging performed
next_followups: ω-runner-13d (deferred): design and implement hexa-daemon (fork-isolated or module-reset model). Validate with isolated-state test before any bridge_health.sh wiring.

ω-runner-13e (deferred, after 13d): wire bridge_health.sh sweep through hexa-daemon; measure end-to-end wall-time; target ≥10× sweep speedup vs current ~16 × 35ms = ~560ms hexa-startup floor (plus bridge selftest body, which dominates wall and is NOT amortized).
ω-hexa-batch: if/when `hexa batch` (already in --help) is implemented in hexa-lang, revisit bridge_health.sh — `hexa batch` may be the cleanest path with no daemon overhead.

investigate whether bridge selftest body times (network latency to remote APIs) dominate the 560ms hexa-startup share — if selftest body wall is e.g. 5-15s/sweep then a 560ms amortization is <10% relief and the brief's ≥10× target is unreachable regardless of daemon design (re-read the brief's target before committing 13d/13e)
honesty_note: The brief's framing assumed 13b's speedup was a portable optimization that only needed integration. On closer look, 13b's speedup is conditional on `every call going through docker exec`. bridge_health.sh does not. The ≥10× sweep speedup target may not even be physically achievable via persistent FIFO regardless of integration effort, because the sweep's wall is dominated by per-bridge selftest body (network I/O to upstream APIs), NOT by per-call invocation overhead. A future cycle should first profile bridge_health.sh sweep to see what fraction of wall is invocation vs body — if body dominates, no invocation-side optimization can hit ≥10×.
